summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers are too obsessed with technical metrics like code coverage while missing the bigger picture: whether users actually want to use their software. He references Alan Cooper's keynote about how interaction designers are marginalized by developers who control the code but often create poor user experiences. Atwood urges programmers to think beyond technical correctness and embrace interaction design principles. The ultimate measure of software quality isn't test coverage—it's user adoption and usability.
key_points: Code coverage and unit tests are valuable but meaningless if users won't or can't use the application
Alan Cooper describes programmers as 'Stay-Puft Marshmallow Man' barriers to good interaction design
Interaction designers are marginalized because they don't control the code, even though they understand user needs
Programmers need to learn interaction design principles and respond to user research rather than dismissing it
The real 'unit test' that matters is whether users choose to use your application
money_quotes: When users can't figure out how to use your app, when users pass over your app in favor of something easier or simpler to use, that's the ultimate unit test failure.
I want to run up to my fellow programmers and physically shake them: think bigger!
Cooper considers most programmers twenty-story marshmallow barriers to good interaction design.
We don't need to change interaction design; we need to re-orient organizations to build things right.
Keep your unit testing and code coverage in perspective – the ultimate unit test is whether or not users want to use your application.
All the other tests you write are totally irrelevant until you can get that one to pass.
key_insight: Technical excellence in code is worthless if it doesn't serve users—the only test that truly matters is whether people want to use what you've built.
